From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

In [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), video games are considered to be a major [social activity](/wiki/Social_relation "Social relation"), with most of the games being [cooperative](/wiki/Cooperative_video_game "Cooperative video game") or [competitive](/wiki/Category:Competitive_video_gaming "Category:Competitive video gaming"). Locally developed [role-playing](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game"), [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter"), [MMORPG](/wiki/MMORPG "MMORPG") and [mobile](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") games have proven to be very popular in the country. [Professional competition](/wiki/Electronic_sports "Electronic sports") surrounding video games (especially those involving [real-time strategy](/wiki/Real-time_strategy "Real-time strategy") games) also enjoy a substantial following in South Korea—major tournaments are often broadcast on television and have large prizes available.

[South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") has developed a strong economy in Asia through the development of creative industries (i.e. Online Game).[[1]](#cite_note-1) *[New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* culture writer Seth Schiesel has commented "When it comes to gaming, Korea is the developed market... When you look at gaming around the world, Korea is the leader in many ways..."[[2]](#cite_note-2) Statistic provided by Korea Creative Content Agency shows that the industry has gained an average growth of 14.9% in sales since 2008.[[3]](#cite_note-3) This statistic may reflects an increasing interest in online gaming, especially the youth. Although it is difficult to mark an exact period that is responsible for increasing trend in online gaming; however, it is quite clear that gaming has become much more than activity for leisure.

South Korea has been known for their pre-eminent infrastructure in video gaming, and their dominance in [eSports](/wiki/ESports "ESports") scenes. Many of the best video game players and coaches in the world were trained or originated from South Korea, and the country's pro leagues and tournaments across numerous video games are often acclaimed by many to be the "most prestigious and competitive".[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1980s–1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1980s–1990s")]

See also: [History of Eastern role-playing video games](/wiki/History_of_Eastern_role-playing_video_games "History of Eastern role-playing video games") and [History of massively multiplayer online games](/wiki/History_of_massively_multiplayer_online_games "History of massively multiplayer online games")

In January 1975, three units of the relabeled [Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong") machine Computer TV were installed in the Midopa Department Store in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"). The newspaper explained it as a "TV game" and said that big companies such as [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") and Goldstar (now [LG](/wiki/LG_Corp "LG Corp")) were producing new machines, most of them Pong clones. Until the end of the 1970s, "electronic entertainment rooms" quickly spread around the country, despite fierce opposition by conservative parents, media and the regime. By 1980, only 43 arcade establishments were government-approved, while many hundreds were opened illegally.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The Korean video game industry started as mostly an import market, getting machines from [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and [the USA](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America"). Since it didn't have any form of [localization](/wiki/Language_localization "Language localization"), the arcade manufacturers would put names in [Hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul"), making some name changes such as "[Donkey Kong](/wiki/Donkey_Kong "Donkey Kong")" becoming "[King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong "King Kong")".[[7]](#cite_note-hisp1-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Eventually, Korean companies started to develop their own arcade games. One of the first arcade games to be developed by a Korean company is Goindol, released in 1987 by SunA. It was also the first one to be released outside of Korea, with the company Sharp Image Electronics licensing it for North American distribution.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Sharp Image licensed three other arcade games from SunA and Philko for release in North America, and also licensed [Kaneko's](/wiki/Kaneko "Kaneko") [Air Buster](/wiki/Air_Buster "Air Buster") for release in North America. In 2001, a company called GameVision licensed six arcade games from Expotato, [Andamiro](/w/index.php?title=Andamiro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Andamiro (page does not exist)"), SemiCom, and Excellent Soft Design for release in North America.[[10]](#cite_note-10) GameVision also developed a [Game Boy Advance](/wiki/Game_Boy_Advance "Game Boy Advance") port of Tang Tang, which was released by [Take-Two Interactive](/wiki/Take-Two_Interactive "Take-Two Interactive").

Home computers were a luxury import in Korea in the late 1970s and software programming was the domain of institutes like *KIST*.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 1983, domestic computers – which were clones of Japanese and American models – started being distributed as well as computer magazines. In March of the same year, companies like Samsung started to offer computers to schools to raise a computer-savvy generation. These same companies would host software competitions, but most of the programmers that won those competitions developing games preferred to use their knowledge for more serious software or jobs. In 1984, the computer models became more standardized, with almost all new models based on either MSX or Apple II standard. This made it easier to import and copy foreign games, as there was no [copyright law in Korea](/wiki/Copyright_law_of_South_Korea "Copyright law of South Korea") at the time for computer programs.[[7]](#cite_note-hisp1-7)

In December 1985, [Daewoo](/wiki/Daewoo "Daewoo") released the [Zemmix](/wiki/Zemmix "Zemmix"), a MSX-based video game console. It was the first domestic gaming hardware success, owing its sales to the huge number of imported and bootlegged games available. Because of that, domestic game development wasn't seen as necessary until July 1987, when a law protecting copyright ownership of computer programs was enacted. This led to the creation of small businesses with the intention of producing and publishing games. The country's first fully-fledged computer game was *Sin'geom-ui Jeonseol*, also known as *Legend of the Sword*, released for the Apple II computer platform in 1987. It was programmed by Nam In-Hwan and distributed by [Aproman](/w/index.php?title=Aproman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aproman (page does not exist)"), being primarily influenced by the *[Ultima](/wiki/Ultima_(series) "Ultima (series)")* series.[[7]](#cite_note-hisp1-7)

Most of the stores that made unauthorized copies of games started to port them to Zemmix, the most representative publisher being [Zemina](/wiki/Zemina "Zemina"), the first company to publish a domestic title, *Brother Adventure*, a [Mario Bros.](/wiki/Mario_Bros. "Mario Bros.") clone. However, the copyright law only covered the code itself, allowing the [video game adaptation](/wiki/Porting#Porting_in_gaming "Porting") of foreign games. A group of Japanese companies (including [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito"), [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami") and [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")) brought to court cases against Haitai and Young Toys, but failed to win anything because the games in question were released before the enactment of the law. Most of the original Korean games were made by [independent teams](/wiki/Independent_video_game_development "Independent video game development"), such as "Mickey Soft's Kkoedori" and "New Age Team's Legendly Night". The Korean company Topia was one of the first to begin producing [action role-playing games](/wiki/Action_role-playing_game "Action role-playing game"), one of which was *Pungnyu Hyeopgaek*, for [MS-DOS](/wiki/MS-DOS "MS-DOS"), in 1989. It was the first Korean title published for an [IBM PC compatible](/wiki/IBM_PC_compatible "IBM PC compatible") and set in [ancient China](/wiki/Ancient_China "Ancient China").[[7]](#cite_note-hisp1-7)

Foreign companies like [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") and [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") had difficulty entering the market, so they licensed out their consoles to Korean companies. Samsung took Sega's [Master System](/wiki/Master_System "Master System"), which was then released in April 1989 as the "Samsung Gam\*Boy". Most of the games were released on Korea on their original languages, being *[Phantasy Star](/wiki/Phantasy_Star_(video_game) "Phantasy Star (video game)")* the first game to be fully translated to [Hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul"). One year later, the [Mega Drive](/wiki/Mega_Drive "Mega Drive") arrived with the name of "Super Gam\*Boy", having in 1992 all Samsung consoles renamed to "Aladdin Boy". Samsung also produced its own game, a [shoot 'em up](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up") called "Uju Geobukseon". [Hyundai](/wiki/Hyundai_Group "Hyundai Group") was the responsible for the releasing the [NES](/wiki/NES "NES"), named Comboy. However, it didn't have any translated games.[[7]](#cite_note-hisp1-7)

The development of those systems started slow, as the software necessary was not as available as home computers. Most infringing companies found ways to simply convert MSX games to the Gam\*Boy, due to their similar architecture. Two companies, Daou Infosys and Open Production, under the Jaem Jaem Club label, were responsible for a steady flow of domestic games for Gam\*Boy consoles. Daou was known for its licensed game from the TV animation series *Agi Gongnyong Dooly*, which had a game released for the MSX. Open Production, on the other hand, was mainly responsible for original games, although most of them were platformers similar to other famous games, however, having completely original sprites, levels and gameplay. Three Open Production games were published in Australia, but only in 1995 when the Master System was already dead in Korea.[[7]](#cite_note-hisp1-7)

By 1990, the excitement for games developed in Korea declined. The lack of skill, budget, and manpower made it hard for the domestic developers to compete with imported games from Japan and the U.S. However, PC games started to rise. Until 1992, most of the games for PC were ports or adaptations of traditional [boardgames](/wiki/Board_game "Board game") or [card games](/wiki/Card_game "Card game"). When computers able to display colored graphics became more common, the industry started to produce games that could compete with consoles on the international market. Big companies started to invest in the development of games and Goldstar opened an educational institute for game developers on March 8, 1993. Localization of the games to the Korean language also became more frequent.[[7]](#cite_note-hisp1-7)

Two major Korean RPGs released in 1994: *[Astonishia Story](/wiki/Astonishia_Story "Astonishia Story")*, and an [MS-DOS](/wiki/MS-DOS "MS-DOS") [enhanced remake](/wiki/Video_game_remake "Video game remake") *Ys II Special*, developed by Mantra. The latter was a mash-up of [Nihon Falcom](/wiki/Nihon_Falcom "Nihon Falcom")'s game *[Ys II](/wiki/Ys_II "Ys II")* (1988) with the [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") *[Ys II: Castle in the Heavens](/wiki/Ys_(anime) "Ys (anime)")* (1992) along with a large amount of new content, including more secrets than any other version of *Ys II*. Both games were a success in Korea.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Commercial [online gaming](/wiki/Online_game "Online game") became very popular in South Korea from the mid-1990s. *[Nexus: The Kingdom of the Winds](/wiki/Nexus:_The_Kingdom_of_the_Winds "Nexus: The Kingdom of the Winds")*, designed by [Jake Song](/w/index.php?title=Jake_Song&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jake Song (page does not exist)"), was commercially released in 1996 and eventually gained over one million subscribers. It was one of the earliest [massively multiplayer online role-playing games](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_role-playing_game "Massively multiplayer online role-playing game"). Song's next game, *[Lineage](/wiki/Lineage_(computer_game) "Lineage (computer game)")* (1998), enjoyed even greater success gaining millions of subscribers in Korea and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").

During February 24 to 27, 1993, Computer Edutainment and Game Software Festival - the first video game expo in Korea - was held at the electronic store complex in Yongsan, Seoul.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The first edition of the festival had high-profile exhibitors such as Hyundai, but on the following years, only small developers would continue to carry it on until its extinction in 1996. On the other hand, the Amuse World expo started as a small event and kept growing steadily, evolving to the nowadays [G-Star](/wiki/G-Star "G-Star"), the largest game industry event in Korea.[[7]](#cite_note-hisp1-7)

### Home console predominance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Home console predominance")]

Around January 1993, home consoles in South Korea were estimated to be present in one of every four houses.[[15]](#cite_note-15) However, they are not as popular as they used to be. The console downfall started with a [photosensitive epileptic](/wiki/Photosensitive_epilepsy "Photosensitive epilepsy") [seizure](/wiki/Seizure "Seizure") [mass hysteria](/wiki/Mass_hysteria "Mass hysteria") successfully spread by the Korean mass media. Although the initial epileptic seizure was proven not to be related to flashing light sensitivity,[[16]](#cite_note-16) the newspapers would report new or old cases, connecting them with video games. The media would blame Japanese video games, even stating that the cases happening in the US and Canada were also caused only by video games from Japan. Video game sales were damaged, and Samsung reported a decrease of 71.4% during 1993 and Hyundai, 33%.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The industry started to slowly recuperate but was slowed down by the decision of the Ministry of Culture and Sports, on July 1, 1993, to revise the censorship regulation, so that video games on CD-ROM or cartridge have to pass an evaluation by the Korea Public Performance Ethics Committee. The rating system of the committee was considered one of the most strict of the world in the 1990s.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### 2000–2009: Korean online gaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2000–2009: Korean online gaming")]

On November 11, 2001, the [sprite](/wiki/Sprite_(computer_graphics) "Sprite (computer graphics)")-based *[Ragnarok Online](/wiki/Ragnarok_Online "Ragnarok Online")*, produced by Korean company [Gravity Corp](/wiki/Gravity_(Korean_company) "Gravity (Korean company)"), was released.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Though unknown to many Western players, the game took Asia by storm as *Lineage* had done. The publisher has claimed in excess of 25 million subscribers of the game, although this number is based upon a number of registered users (rather than active subscribers).[[20]](#cite_note-20) 2002 also saw the release of *[MapleStory](/wiki/MapleStory "MapleStory")*, another sprite-based title, which was completely [free-to-play](/wiki/Free-to-play "Free-to-play") - instead of charging a monthly fee, it generated revenue by selling in-game "enhancements". *MapleStory* would go on to become a major player in the new market for free-to-play MMORPGs (generating huge numbers of registered accounts across its many versions), if it did not introduce the market by itself.

In October 2003, *[Lineage II](/wiki/Lineage_II "Lineage II")* (NCsoft's sequel to *Lineage*) became the latest MMORPG to achieve huge success across Asia. It received the Presidential Award at the 2003 Korean Game awards and is now the second most popular MMORPG in the world. As of the first half of 2005 *Lineage II* counted over 2.25 million subscribers worldwide, with servers in Japan, China, North America, Taiwan, and Europe, once the popularity of the game had surged in the West. All the objectives that are included in Lineage II started to take shape following the work of designing the original mythology.

### 2009–present: Transition to mobile platform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2009–present: Transition to mobile platform")]

After the release of the iPhone, games like *[Angry Birds](/wiki/Angry_Birds "Angry Birds")* showed up on the market, showing off their success. Like that, in South Korea, the major game company [Com2uS](/wiki/Com2uS "Com2uS") and [Gamevil](/wiki/Gamevil "Gamevil") started to release their new games, *[Home Run Battle 3D](/wiki/Home_Run_Battle_3D "Home Run Battle 3D")* and *[ZENONIA](/wiki/ZENONIA "ZENONIA")*, on the market in 2009.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2012, [Kakao](/wiki/Kakao "Kakao") launched their new service called *[Kakao Games](/wiki/Kakao_Games "Kakao Games")* and they released their first game, *[Anipang](/wiki/Anipang "Anipang")*, which was a huge success to both Kakao and its developer, [SundayToz](/w/index.php?title=SundayToz&action=edit&redlink=1 "SundayToz (page does not exist)"). After the launch of Kakao Games, major video game companies like [Nexon](/wiki/Nexon "Nexon"), [Netmarble](/wiki/Netmarble "Netmarble"), and many other minor game developers began to give their attention to mobile platform.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2016, Netmarble released their new [MMORPG](/wiki/MMORPG "MMORPG") game, *[Lineage 2 Revolution](/wiki/Lineage_2_Revolution "Lineage 2 Revolution")*, by using *[Lineage](/wiki/Lineage_(series) "Lineage (series)")*'s IP. The game grossed ₩206.5 million in one month, and it became a trend to make a mobile game based on a popular online game, such as *[Lineage](/wiki/Lineage_(video_game) "Lineage (video game)")*, *[Black Desert Online](/wiki/Black_Desert_Online "Black Desert Online")*, and *[Tera Online](/wiki/Tera_Online "Tera Online")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Recently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] in South Korea, gamers have been pessimistic about the video game industry in South Korea, saying the industry is in its Dark Ages. Many companies[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] are still making mobile games, mostly, and focusing more on advertising than actual gameplay.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## PC bangs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: PC bangs")]

Main article: [PC bang](/wiki/PC_bang "PC bang")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Korean.culture-PC.bang-01.jpg/330px-Korean.culture-PC.bang-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Korean.culture-PC.bang-01.jpg)

Korean PC bang

A **PC bang** ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"): PC방; literally "[PC](/wiki/Personal_Computer "Personal Computer") room") is a type of [LAN gaming center](/wiki/LAN_gaming_center "LAN gaming center"), where patrons can play [multiplayer](/wiki/Multiplayer "Multiplayer") [computer games](/wiki/Computer_games "Computer games") and browse the internet for a small hourly fee. The typical cost for an hour of play ranges from 1,000 to 1,500 [won](/wiki/South_Korean_won "South Korean won") (approximately $0.90 to $1.35 [USD](/wiki/US_Dollars "US Dollars")); however, as of 2013, 1,200 won per hour is the most common cost in PC bang.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Although the per capita penetration of computers and [broadband internet access](/wiki/Broadband_internet_access "Broadband internet access") is very high in South Korea, PC bangs remain popular as they provide a social meeting place for gamers (especially school-aged gamers) to play together with their friends. Furthermore, the [computer hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware") used by PC bangs may be more powerful than the systems available in the players' homes. Most PC bangs allow players to eat, drink and smoke (often with separate smoking and non-smoking sections) while they play. It is common for PC bangs to sell [ramen noodles](/wiki/Ramen_noodles "Ramen noodles"), canned coffee, [soft drinks](/wiki/Soft_drinks "Soft drinks"), and other snacks.

PC bangs rose to popularity following the release of the PC game *[StarCraft](/wiki/StarCraft "StarCraft")* in 1998.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Although PC bangs are used by all ages and genders, they are most popular with male gamers in their teens and twenties.[[23]](#cite_note-kt-23)

Many popular Korean multiplayer games provide players with incentives that encourage them to play from a PC bang. For example, the [Nexon](/wiki/Nexon "Nexon") games [Kart Rider](/wiki/Kart_Rider "Kart Rider") and [BnB](/wiki/PopTag "PopTag") reward players with bonus "Lucci"—the games' virtual currencies—when they log on from a PC bang and the popular *[League of Legends](/wiki/League_of_Legends "League of Legends")* provides free access to all characters and extra game currency on each match.

## Professional and competitive gaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Professional and competitive gaming")]

See also: [Electronic sports](/wiki/Electronic_sports "Electronic sports") and [Professional StarCraft competition](/wiki/Professional_StarCraft_competition "Professional StarCraft competition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Esportstadium.jpg/330px-Esportstadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Esportstadium.jpg)

Korean E-Sports Stadium at Yongsan I'Park Mall

[South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") is well known for the fact that [professional gaming](/wiki/Professional_gaming "Professional gaming") has a very substantial following in the country, with the top players earning big money prizes in competitions, and spending a significant number of hours practicing every day.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Pro-gaming tournaments in South Korea are broadcast, with millions of people tuning in to watch live or catch the results [[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) on one of three channels that are exclusively geared toward [e-sports](/wiki/ESports "ESports").[[26]](#cite_note-:1-26) In South Korea, pro-gaming and e-sports competitions are considered a national pastime with approximately 10 million regular viewers.[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27) There are also organized leagues throughout the country that are financed generously and train gamers to compete in competitions.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25)

Many South Koreans take pride in the country's high standing position as a pro-gaming powerhouse. The results of this are seen in the fact that more and more tech companies are seeing the profitability of investing in and sponsoring e-sport stars.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) Companies are starting to finance the coaching of potential gamers, as it is advantageous to them financially and socially. Major technological companies like HTC [[28]](#cite_note-28) and three of Korea's largest companies - Samsung, Korea Telecom, and SK Telecom are a few examples.[[29]](#cite_note-:3-29) The Korean government has also discovered the promise of investing in e-sports and pro-gaming by funding the world's first e-sports stadium that was built in 2005.[[26]](#cite_note-:1-26) Additionally, the Korean government has a department solely focused on the governing of e-sports known as the [Korea e-Sports Association](/wiki/Korean_e-Sports_Association "Korean e-Sports Association") (KeSpa).[[29]](#cite_note-:3-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Yongsan_e-sports_stadium_hall_of_fame.jpg/330px-Yongsan_e-sports_stadium_hall_of_fame.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yongsan_e-sports_stadium_hall_of_fame.jpg)

Hall of fame located at the Yongsan E-sports Stadium

Throughout South Korea, pro-gamers are revered and treated like celebrities. It is not unheard of for successful pro-gamers or e-sport stars to earn six figure contracts or higher.[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27) In turn the spotlight that these stars shine on video games helps the South Korean economy. The video game industry makes up a significant share of South Korea's GDP.[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27) It is estimated that the game market pulls in approximately 5 billion dollars annually along with the millions of dollars that are also traded in illegal gambling and betting that stimulates South Korea's informal economy.[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27)

Due to the huge popularity of e-sports in South Korea, the [World Cyber Games](/wiki/World_Cyber_Games "World Cyber Games") was made in 2000.[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27) The Republic of Korea's Ministry of Culture and Tourism, Ministry of Information and Communications, Samsung, and Microsoft are the original sponsors. The WCG is considered to be the "Olympics of the online gaming world".[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27) The games draw around 500 pro-gamers from around the world into competition with prizes amassing from $200,000 - $500,000.

Two particularly popular video games for pro-gamers are *[StarCraft](/wiki/StarCraft "StarCraft")* and *[League of Legends](/wiki/League_of_Legends "League of Legends")*. Well-known players include [Lim Yo-Hwan](/wiki/Lim_Yo-Hwan "Lim Yo-Hwan"), [Lee Young-ho](/wiki/Flash_(gamer) "Flash (gamer)"), [Lee Sang-hyeok](/wiki/Faker_(video_gamer) "Faker (video gamer)"), [Choi Yeon-Sung](/wiki/Choi_Yeon-Sung "Choi Yeon-Sung"), [Park Sung-Joon](/wiki/Park_Sung-joon_(electronic_sports) "Park Sung-joon (electronic sports)") and [Lee Jae-Dong](/wiki/Lee_Jae-Dong "Lee Jae-Dong").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Video game addiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Video game addiction")]

With video game addictions, many studies tried to find the connection between video game addiction and psychological conditions like depression and anxiety in Korea. A Korean study reported that there was a connection between video game addiction and constraints involving recreation participation. The study also found that video game addictions were associated with experience in recreational activities as well as family environment. The level of addiction differed depending on family background, family communication, and parental monitoring. To reduce video game addictions, researchers suggested that there should be better family bonding and flexibility, like participating in various recreational activities involving family members.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Due to problems of widespread [video game addiction](/wiki/Video_game_addiction "Video game addiction") threatening the health safety of players and after different incidents related to it,[[32]](#cite_note-32) the Korean government has invested considerable amounts into new clinics, campaigns, and support groups to minimize the problem.[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27) By late 2011, the government took a step further and imposed the "Cinderella Law", also known as the [Shutdown law](/wiki/Shutdown_law "Shutdown law"),[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27) which prevents anyone aged under 16 from playing games online between 10 pm - 6 am.[[33]](#cite_note-33) "Minors are required to register their national identification cards online so that they can be monitored and regulated".[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27) Another program created by the Korean government is the Jump up Internet Rescue School, a camp created to cure children who are either addicted to online games or the internet. This program was created due to the increasing number of working parents, insufficient space for playgrounds, and a highly competitive educational environment. The program involved having a wide variety of treatments for 12 days and 11 nights. The facility will allow participants to engage in outdoor activities and sports instead of playing video games. The program is divided into two stages which are training activities and educational activities. The education activity consists of mental training, brain education about the frontal lobe, emotional control, and brain system training. The role of mental and brain training is to recall the participant's cognitive skills. People who are addictive thinkers are more likely to worry than an average person which may lead to more addictive internet uses. An emotional approach is implemented so that addicts can move on from gratifying sensations and feelings that make addicts come back to gaming or internet use. The four education activities listed previously are important to prevent or detour behaviours that the internet has, such as providing a mental escape, avoiding problems, and emotional belief, in addition to doing drugs, and gambling.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Due to a failure of establishing a clear definition of online video game addiction, there are complications measuring and identifying those affected by video game addiction. There is no actual percentage regarding individuals that are addicted to video games. Researchers have conducted a questionnaire for Korean High school students to better understand video game addiction. The researchers found only a 2.7% addiction rate when it was distinguished from another peripheral criterion. The results suggest that video game addiction may not have been a prevalent issue as previously believed in South Korea[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In a survey that was done in 2018 the South Korean government got a rough estimate that 10 million people were at risk of Internet Addiction.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Video game addiction existed back in the early 2000s; in that decade a South Korean man died from a heart attack after playing video games for 50 hours in a [PC bang](/wiki/PC_bang "PC bang") where he barely slept and ate. He was also fired from his job because he didn't show up to work due to him playing computer games.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Ratings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_South_Korea&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ratings")]

Video games in Korea are rated by the [Game Rating and Administration Committee](/wiki/Game_Rating_and_Administration_Committee "Game Rating and Administration Committee"), a governmental organization established in 2006. Games were previously rated by the [Korea Media Rating Board](/wiki/Korea_Media_Rating_Board "Korea Media Rating Board") (KMRB). A separate board was established in 2006 following a scandal where the KMRB was allegedly bribed to allow a [video slot machine](/wiki/Video_slot_machine "Video slot machine") known as *Sea Story* be put on the market after operators hacked the game to increase its payouts beyond legal limits.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
